one_line_takeaway: Under 1% adversarially poisoned pre-training data, or single-digit-percent poisoned fine-tuning windows, can install silent, trigger-conditioned backdoors in DNA foundation models (Evo 2, GENERator) and can invert a clinical BRCA1 variant classifier's decision boundary (AUROC 0.849 to 0.415) via targeted label corruption -- but installability is gated by corpus token-frequency statistics, as shown by one trigger's complete failure to install under 6-mer tokenization.
key_finding: Backdoors install reliably at pre-training with under 1% cumulative poisoned data: no trigger activated below ~0.35% cumulative poison, but CTCF (the most readily learned trigger) crossed 80% activation by ~0.6% and saturated at 100% by 0.69% in Evo 2, with a comparable trajectory in GENERator (80% near 0.55%, >95% from 0.85%). Poisoned models on trigger-containing prompts showed near-deterministic perplexity collapse (Evo 2: 3.1-3.4 to ~1.008-1.009) while clean-genomic perplexity stayed statistically indistinguishable from the unpoisoned baseline, indicating the attack is silent/selective. Installed backdoors partially generalize to near-trigger mutated sequences (e.g., Evo 2 CTCF backdoor retained 40% activation under a single point mutation, 8% under two; GENERator retained 54% and 27% respectively). Notably, the GENERator TATA-box trigger never installed above noise at any dose 0-2%, which the authors trace via rho (TATA-box rho=8.70e-4 vs. CTCF rho=4.46e-3 and nullomer rho=3.94e-3) and causal attribution to the trigger's terminal 6-mer token (ATATAT) being the third most frequent 6-mer in the entire GENERator corpus (~31 million occurrences), so the adversarial signal was outnumbered by natural supervision by roughly half an order of magnitude relative to the two triggers that did install. In LoRA fine-tuning of Evo 2 7B, activation on exact-trigger prompts rose from 0% at p=0.03 to 11.8% at p=0.05, 76.0% at p=0.10, 88.0% at p=0.15, 91.4% at p=0.20, and 99.6% at p=1.00, while the Clean prompt set never activated (0.0%) at any dose, and Misaligned-CTCF (near-consensus but non-exact triggers) rose only gradually to 32.0% at p=1.00 -- since poisoning is defined over CTCF-overlapping windows only, p=0.05 and p=0.10 correspond to just 1.72% and 3.44% of the total 50,000-window training set. In the BRCA1 label-poisoning experiment, the clean baseline classifier achieved global AUROC 0.886 (BRCT AUROC 0.849, RING AUROC 0.849); flipping all BRCT labels dropped BRCT AUROC to 0.415 (a 43.4 percentage-point decline) while RING AUROC declined only to 0.791 (a ~0.058 spillover), and BRCT AUROC fell below the 0.50 chance level between 80% and 100% poisoning, indicating the classifier learned an inverted decision boundary rather than merely losing discriminative power; a reciprocal control flipping all RING labels instead dropped RING AUROC to 0.649 (milder, attributed to RING's smaller variant count of 795 vs. BRCT's 1,736) while BRCT AUROC declined to 0.789.
core_contribution: The authors present what they describe as the first systematic study of training-data poisoning against genomic (DNA) foundation models, spanning both pre-training and fine-tuning stages. At pre-training they demonstrate trigger-payload backdoor insertion on two architectures (Evo 2, a 100M-parameter character-level model, and GENERator, an 800M-parameter 6-mer-tokenized LLaMA-style model), using three biologically motivated trigger types (TATA-box promoter motif, CTCF binding-site motif, and a synthetic nullomer absent from the training corpus). At fine-tuning they demonstrate (1) a LoRA-based conditional backdoor installed via poisoning a fraction of CTCF-overlapping windows in a ClinVar-derived corpus, and (2) a label-flipping attack on a logistic-regression classifier trained on frozen Evo 2 7B embeddings for BRCA1 variant-effect (LOF vs FUNC) classification. They also provide a mechanistic explanation, via a poison-to-natural exposure ratio and per-token causal (KL-divergence) attribution, for why one specific trigger (TATA-box under GENERator's 6-mer tokenizer) failed to install.
threat_model: The adversary is assumed able to inject a small fraction of adversarially crafted sequences into a public/community-sourced genomic training corpus (pre-training) or into a fine-tuning corpus such as ClinVar-derived variant windows, or to corrupt functional annotation labels used to train a downstream classifier (fine-tuning/label-poisoning setting). The attacker does not control the model architecture or training procedure; the goal is a trigger-conditioned backdoor (silent on clean inputs, activates on trigger-bearing inputs) or a domain-targeted classifier degradation. The paper frames this as realistic because genomic repositories (NCBI RefSeq, GenBank, GTDB, IMG/VR, ClinVar) accept community submissions optimized for throughput rather than adversarial robustness, and because DNA lacks the semantic transparency that would let a human reviewer spot a corrupted or anomalous sequence the way they might catch anomalous text.
limitations: The paper's own text flags several caveats rather than presenting the results as unconditionally general. First, one of five installable pre-training trigger-platform conditions (GENERator TATA-box) failed to install at all, showing the attack's success is contingent on token-frequency statistics under the model's specific tokenizer, not guaranteed for arbitrary triggers. Second, the poly-A payload configurations for TATA-box/CTCF are explicitly described by the authors as 'easier-attack baselines that establish the floor of installability rather than the ceiling of biological consequence,' with the nullomer condition offered as the cleaner evidence of structured payload commitment. Third, near-trigger mutation robustness is only partial and varies substantially by model and trigger (e.g., Evo 2 nullomer backdoor dropped to 0% activation under two-base mutation, while GENERator CTCF retained 27-49%), so the attack's real-world robustness to natural sequence variation is inconsistent rather than uniform. Fourth, the LoRA and label-poisoning experiments use relatively small, curated corpora (a 50,000-window ClinVar-derived set; 3,644 BRCA1 SNVs from one SGE dataset) and a single downstream classifier type (logistic regression on frozen embeddings), leaving open whether results transfer to larger, more heterogeneous fine-tuning corpora or other classifier architectures. The excerpted text (pages 1-22 of the PDF, through the start of the Methods token-attribution subsection) does not include the paper's discussion/limitations section or any defenses/mitigations evaluation beyond the general recommendation for data provenance and integrity verification, so a full accounting of author-stated limitations or defense evaluation cannot be confirmed from the portion reviewed.

datasets_used: OpenGenome2 (subset, ~1.3 trillion bp; ~23 billion bp / ~1.8% used for Evo 2 pre-training) spanning bacteria, archaea, viruses, and eukaryotes

NCBI RefSeq eukaryotic GenBank flat-file (GBFF) records + FASTA for GENERator pre-training (~54.6 million gene records, ~979.8 billion bp, across protozoa, fungi, plant, invertebrate, vertebrate-other, vertebrate-mammalian)
Genome Taxonomy Database (GTDB) and IMG/VR (prokaryotic/viral sequences contributing to OpenGenome2 subset)
ClinVar noncoding variants overlapping CTCF binding sites (fine-tuning corpus for LoRA backdoor experiment, 50,000 training windows)

Findlay et al. BRCA1 saturation genome editing (SGE) dataset: 3,644 single-nucleotide variants (2,821 functional, 823 loss-of-function) across RING domain (795 variants), BRCT domain (1,736 variants), and other exons (1,113 variants)
quantitative_results: Evo 2 pre-training: suffix perplexity on trigger prompts collapses from 3.1-3.4 (clean) to ~1.008-1.009 (poisoned) across TATA-box, CTCF, and nullomer triggers
Backdoor activation crosses 80% by ~0.6% cumulative poison (Evo 2 CTCF) and saturates at 100% by 0.69%; GENERator CTCF crosses 80% near 0.55% and saturates >95% from 0.85%
GENERator TATA-box trigger never activates above noise at any dose 0-2% cumulative poison
Poison-to-natural exposure ratio rho: TATA-box 8.70e-4 (failed), CTCF 4.46e-3, nullomer 3.94e-3 (both installed)
GENERator terminal TATA-box token ATATAT is the 3rd most frequent 6-mer in the corpus (~31 million occurrences) vs. CTCF/nullomer terminal tokens at rank ~10^3 with ~10^6 counts
Near-trigger mutation robustness: Evo 2 CTCF/nullomer backdoors retain 40%/26% activation under 1-base mutation, 8%/0% under 2-base mutation; GENERator retains 54%/86% (1-base) and 27%/49% (2-base)

LoRA fine-tuning (Evo 2 7B, CTCF trigger): activation rate 0.0% (control, p=0.03) -> 11.8% (p=0.05) -> 76.0% (p=0.10) -> 88.0% (p=0.15) -> 91.4% (p=0.20) -> 95.6%/97.2%/99.6% (p=0.40/0.60/1.00); Clean prompts stay at 0.0% at every dose; Misaligned CTCF reaches only 32.0% at p=1.00
p=0.05 and p=0.10 (fraction of CTCF-overlapping windows) correspond to only 1.72% and 3.44% of the total 50,000-window fine-tuning corpus
BRCA1 classifier clean baseline: global AUROC 0.886, BRCT AUROC 0.849, RING AUROC 0.849

100% BRCT label flip: BRCT AUROC collapses to 0.415 (43.4 pp drop, below chance), RING AUROC declines only to 0.791; global AUROC falls to 0.661 at 100% BRCT poisoning (~48% of all training labels corrupted)
100% RING label flip (reciprocal control): RING AUROC drops to 0.649, BRCT AUROC declines to 0.789
baselines_compared: Clean (unpoisoned) Evo 2 100M and GENERator 800M pre-trained models as perplexity/composition baselines
Clean LoRA-fine-tuned Evo 2 7B checkpoint (p=0 control) for CTCF backdoor activation rate
Clean-baseline (0% label-poisoning) BRCA1 logistic regression classifier on frozen Evo 2 7B embeddings
Cross-poisoning control comparing BRCT-targeted vs. RING-targeted label flipping to establish domain-specificity of the label-poisoning attack

BadNets (Carlini et al., Souly et al., Hubinger et al., Alber et al.) cited as prior LLM/CV/biomedical poisoning literature establishing the general threat, but these are related work, not the paper's own experiments
